description: A structured review using pre-defined eligibility criteria to identify, appraise, and synthesize all relevant studies on a specific question, following PRISMA reporting guidelines.
when_to_use: When you need a comprehensive, reproducible summary of all existing evidence on a focused research question; standard for medical and policy decision-making.
seeks_refutation: Yes — explicit inclusion/exclusion criteria and critical appraisal seek to minimize bias and identify weak studies.
source_name: PRISMA 2020 Statement (Page et al., 2021, BMJ)

description: A systematic mapping of the breadth and key concepts of a research area, typically following the Arksey & O'Malley (2005) framework or JBI methodology. Addresses exploratory questions rather than testing hypotheses.
when_to_use: When the research area is complex, heterogeneous, or emerging, and you need to clarify concepts, identify knowledge gaps, or determine whether a full systematic review is warranted.
seeks_refutation: Partially — seeks to identify gaps and map coverage rather than formally test or refute a hypothesis.
source_name: Arksey & O'Malley (2005) framework; PRISMA-ScR extension for scoping reviews

description: A form of knowledge synthesis in which components of the systematic review process are simplified or omitted to produce information in a timely manner (Khangura et al., 2012).
when_to_use: When time-sensitive evidence is needed for policy or clinical decisions and a full systematic review is not feasible due to resource or time constraints.
seeks_refutation: Yes — still applies critical appraisal but with acknowledged limitations in search breadth and depth.
source_name: Khangura et al. (2012) Evidence summaries: the evolution of a rapid review approach — Systematic Reviews journal

description: An empirical inquiry that investigates a contemporary phenomenon within its real-life context, especially when boundaries between phenomenon and context are not clearly evident (Yin, 2018).
when_to_use: When you want an in-depth understanding of a complex phenomenon in its real-world setting; useful for answering 'how' and 'why' questions.
seeks_refutation: No — primarily seeks to build or refine theory through detailed description and explanation, not falsification.
source_name: Robert K. Yin (2018) Case Study Research and Applications: Design and Methods (6th ed.)

description: An inductive theory-building method where theory is generated systematically from data collected and analyzed through constant comparison and theoretical sampling (Glaser & Strauss, 1967).
when_to_use: When no existing theory adequately explains a phenomenon and you want to build a new theory grounded in empirical data collected from the field.
seeks_refutation: No — seeks to construct theory from data rather than test or refute a pre-existing hypothesis; emphasizes emergent theory.
source_name: Barney G. Glaser & Anselm L. Strauss (1967) The Discovery of Grounded Theory: Strategies for Qualitative Research

description: A qualitative method rooted in anthropology involving prolonged immersion in a community or setting to understand cultural patterns, meanings, and practices from an emic (insider) perspective via participant observation.
when_to_use: When you need a deep, contextual understanding of a culture, community, or social practice that cannot be captured by surveys or experiments.
seeks_refutation: No — primarily descriptive and interpretive; does not seek to refute hypotheses but to illuminate meaning.
source_name: Yale University HRAF — Introduction to Fieldwork and Ethnography by Francine Barone

description: A structured method of data collection using standardized questionnaires administered to a sample of a population, designed following principles such as Dillman's Total Design Method (1978) to maximize response rates and data quality.
when_to_use: When you need to describe characteristics, attitudes, or behaviors of a large population quickly and cost-effectively; useful for estimating prevalence and correlations.
seeks_refutation: Partially — can be used to test hypotheses, but emphasis is on measurement and description; refutation depends on study design.
source_name: Don A. Dillman (1978) The Total Design Method for mail and telephone surveys

description: A randomized controlled experiment comparing two or more variants (A and B) to determine which produces a better outcome, widely used in digital products, marketing, and clinical trials.
when_to_use: When you can randomly assign users or subjects to treatment groups and need a causal estimate of an intervention's effect on a specific metric.
seeks_refutation: Yes — explicit null hypothesis significance testing (NHST) with p-values and confidence intervals seeks to reject or fail to reject the null hypothesis.
source_name: Alex Deng — Statistical Analysis of A/B Tests (Causal Inference and Its Applications in Online Industry)

description: A research design that estimates causal impact of an intervention without random assignment, using techniques like regression discontinuity, interrupted time series, and nonequivalent control groups (Shadish, Cook & Campbell, 2002).
when_to_use: When random assignment is infeasible or unethical but you still need to estimate a causal effect; common in education, public health, and social policy.
seeks_refutation: Yes — explicitly addresses threats to causal inference (internal validity) and seeks to rule out alternative explanations.
source_name: Shadish, Cook & Campbell (2002) Experimental and Quasi-Experimental Designs for Generalized Causal Inference

description: A structured approach that evaluates two or more methods, theories, or cases against shared criteria to identify similarities, differences, and contextual fit (Sartori, 1994).
when_to_use: When you need to justify a choice among alternatives, explain trade-offs, or demonstrate why one approach performs differently under specific conditions.
seeks_refutation: Yes — comparison against shared criteria can reveal weaknesses and disconfirm claims about superiority.
source_name: Comparative Research Methods (Esser, in Wiley Encyclopedia of Research Methods)

description: A statistical technique combining results from multiple independent studies to produce a pooled effect size estimate, increasing statistical power and resolving conflicting findings. Coined by Gene Glass in 1976.
when_to_use: When multiple studies on the same question yield varying results and you need a statistically rigorous pooled estimate of the overall effect.
seeks_refutation: Yes — explicit hypothesis testing (e.g., homogeneity of effect sizes) and identification of outliers seeks to detect and resolve contradictions.
source_name: Gene V. Glass (1976) Primary, Secondary, and Meta-Analysis of Research — American Educational Research Association presidential address

description: A structured communication technique using iterative rounds of anonymous expert surveys with controlled feedback to achieve consensus on a complex problem, originally developed at the RAND Corporation in the 1950s.
when_to_use: When no empirical evidence can settle a question, expert judgment is needed, and direct face-to-face meetings may be biased by group dynamics.
seeks_refutation: Partially — anonymous iteration allows experts to revise views in light of dissent, but it seeks convergence rather than refutation.
source_name: Linstone & Turoff (2002) The Delphi Method: Techniques and Applications; RAND Corporation origins

description: The use of multiple methods, data sources, theories, or investigators to study the same phenomenon in order to enhance validity, mitigate bias, and corroborate findings (Denzin, 1978).
when_to_use: When findings from a single method or source may be questioned and you need to cross-validate by combining qualitative and quantitative evidence or multiple independent datasets.
seeks_refutation: Yes — convergence across independent methods strengthens confidence; divergence actively exposes weaknesses and potential error in any single method.
source_name: Norman K. Denzin (1978) The Research Act — cited in JSTOR and Walden University review

description: Structured ideation technique using 7 creative operators: Substitute, Combine, Adapt, Modify, Put to another use, Eliminate, Reverse. Based on Osborn (1953) brainstorming principles, formalized by Eberle (1996). Used in Cariak pitching phase.
when_to_use: When exploring a problem space from multiple angles before narrowing to research questions. Best for early-stage, divergent thinking.
seeks_refutation: No — generates breadth, not refutation.
source_name: Eberle, B. (1996). Scamper: Games for Imagination Development. Prufrock Press.

description: Root-cause analysis method from Toyota Production System (Ohno, 1988). Iteratively ask 'Why?' five times to trace from symptom to root cause. Used in Cariak pitching phase.
when_to_use: When the problem appears vague and needs root-cause decomposition before research begins.
seeks_refutation: Yes — each 'Why?' challenges the previous answer, recursively seeking deeper causes.
source_name: Ohno, T. (1988). Toyota Production System: Beyond Large-Scale Production. Productivity Press.

description: Human-centered problem-framing methodology: Empathize → Define → Ideate → Prototype → Test. Popularized by IDEO and Stanford d.school. Used in Cariak pitching phase for wicked/ambiguous problems.
when_to_use: When the research problem is ill-defined, human-centered, or requires stakeholder empathy before formulation.
seeks_refutation: Partially — prototyping and testing phases seek to invalidate assumptions, but the overall flow is generative.
source_name: Brown, T. (2008). Design Thinking. Harvard Business Review, 86(6), 84-92.

description: Structured question framework: Population/Problem, Intervention, Comparison, Outcome. Extensions: PICOC (+Context), SPIDER (Sample, Phenomenon of Interest, Design, Evaluation, Research type). Used in Cariak grinding phase.
when_to_use: When converting broad research directions into specific, testable, answerable research questions.
seeks_refutation: Yes — forces specification of comparison and outcome, making questions falsifiable.
source_name: Richardson, W.S., et al. (1995). The well-built clinical question. ACP Journal Club, 123(3), A12-A13.

description: Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic review and Meta-Analysis Protocols: 17-item checklist for planning and registering systematic review protocols before execution. Used in Cariak grinding phase.
when_to_use: When developing a research protocol that must be reproducible, transparent, and pre-registered.
seeks_refutation: Yes — protocol pre-registration prevents post-hoc bias; checklist items enforce methodological rigor.
source_name: Moher, D., et al. (2015). PRISMA-P 2015 statement. Systematic Reviews, 4(1), 1.

description: Guidelines for performing Systematic Literature Reviews in software engineering: formulate questions → search strategy → source selection → inclusion/exclusion → quality assessment → data extraction → synthesis. Used in Cariak planning phase.
when_to_use: When designing a reproducible search strategy across multiple databases and source types.
seeks_refutation: Yes — explicit eligibility criteria and quality assessment seek to minimize selection bias.
source_name: Kitchenham, B., & Charters, S. (2007). Guidelines for performing Systematic Literature Reviews in Software Engineering. EBSE Technical Report EBSE-2007-01.

description: Open Source Intelligence methodology: Direction → Collection → Processing → Analysis → Dissemination. Multi-source across surface web, deep web, social media, academic, news, market. Used in Cariak researching phase.
when_to_use: When collecting evidence from diverse public/open sources across multiple domains in parallel.
seeks_refutation: Yes — processing and analysis phases verify and cross-reference findings; collection phase documents gaps.
source_name: NATO (2001). Open Source Intelligence Handbook; Williams, H.J., & Blum, I. (2018). Defining Second Generation OSINT. RAND Corporation.

description: Structured database search methodology from Cochrane Handbook Chapter 4: design search strategy → select databases → run queries → deduplicate → screen by title/abstract → screen full-text → extract data. Used in Cariak researching phase.
when_to_use: When executing reproducible, comprehensive database searches across multiple academic and grey literature sources.
seeks_refutation: Yes — systematic search minimizes retrieval bias; screening against pre-defined criteria excludes irrelevant studies.
source_name: Higgins, J.P.T., & Green, S. (2011). Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions, Chapter 4. Wiley.

description: Qualitative synthesis method: line-by-line coding of findings → develop descriptive themes → generate analytical themes → map themes to research questions. Endorsed by Cochrane Qualitative Methods Group. Used in Cariak synthesizing phase.
when_to_use: When integrating findings from heterogeneous sources (qualitative and quantitative) into coherent, traceable themes.
seeks_refutation: Partially — identifies gaps and thin evidence, but primarily aggregates rather than refutes.
source_name: Thomas, J., & Harden, A. (2008). Methods for the thematic synthesis of qualitative research in systematic reviews. BMC Medical Research Methodology, 8(1), 45.

description: Best fit" framework approach: select an a-priori conceptual framework from literature → index findings against it → thematic analysis of data not captured → produce refined framework. Used in Cariak synthesizing phase."
when_to_use: When a relevant conceptual framework already exists and you need to test and extend it with new evidence.
seeks_refutation: Yes — the a-priori framework is explicitly tested against new evidence; uncaptured data reveals gaps.
source_name: Carroll, C., et al. (2013). Best fit" framework synthesis: refining the method. BMC Medical Research Methodology

description: US Government Accountability Office methodology for systematic technology evaluation: describe technology → assess TRL/maturity → evaluate opportunities/challenges → analyze policy implications → review alternatives → produce structured report. Used in Cariak output phase.
when_to_use: When producing implementation-ready technical reports that assess technology maturity, feasibility, and alternatives.
seeks_refutation: Yes — alternatives analysis and risk assessment explicitly challenge the primary technology option.
source_name: U.S. GAO (2021). Technology Assessment Design Handbook. GAO-21-347G.

description: US Army Corps of Engineers structured feasibility methodology: problem statement → objectives/constraints → alternatives analysis → technical assessment → cost-benefit → risk analysis → environmental review → recommendation. Used in Cariak output phase.
when_to_use: When producing a multi-dimensional viability assessment (technical, economic, operational, schedule, risk).
seeks_refutation: Yes — explicit alternatives comparison and risk analysis challenge the recommended option.
source_name: USACE (2023). Feasibility Report Format and Content Guide. ER 1105-2-100.

description: Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation: rates evidence quality as High/Moderate/Low/Very Low based on risk of bias, imprecision, inconsistency, indirectness, publication bias. Adopted by WHO, Cochrane, NICE. Used in Cariak validating phase.
when_to_use: When grading the certainty of evidence for each key claim in a research report.
seeks_refutation: Yes — every domain explicitly searches for reasons to downgrade evidence quality.
source_name: Guyatt, G.H., et al. (2008). GRADE: an emerging consensus on rating quality of evidence. BMJ, 336(7650), 924-926.

description: Critical Appraisal Skills Programme (UK NHS): 10-12 structured questions per study type assessing (A) validity, (B) results, (C) local applicability. Variants for Systematic Reviews, RCTs, Qualitative, Cohort, Case-Control, Economic. Used in Cariak validating phase.
when_to_use: When performing structured critical appraisal of individual source quality across different study designs.
seeks_refutation: Yes — questions explicitly probe methodological weaknesses and threats to validity.
source_name: Critical Appraisal Skills Programme (2018). CASP Checklists. UK National Health Service. casp-uk.net.

description: A MeaSurement Tool to Assess systematic Reviews: 16-item checklist covering PICO, protocol registration, search comprehensiveness, duplicate selection, data extraction, risk of bias, synthesis methods, publication bias, conflict of interest. Used in Cariak reflecting phase.
when_to_use: When assessing the methodological quality of a completed research review before delivery.
seeks_refutation: Yes — each item identifies a specific methodological weakness; failure on critical items invalidates the review.
source_name: Shea, B.J., et al. (2017). AMSTAR 2: a critical appraisal tool for systematic reviews. BMJ, 358, j4008.

description: 27-item reporting completeness standard covering title, abstract, introduction, methods, results, discussion, and other information. Updated from PRISMA 2009. Used in Cariak reflecting phase.
when_to_use: When verifying that a research report meets minimum reporting standards for transparency and reproducibility.
seeks_refutation: Partially — ensures completeness of reporting rather than validity of findings, but missing items flag potential bias.
source_name: Page, M.J., et al. (2021). The PRISMA 2020 statement. BMJ, 372, n71.

description: Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable: DOIs, rich metadata, open formats, clear provenance, persistent repositories. Used in Cariak closing phase to ensure research artifacts remain usable long-term.
when_to_use: When archiving completed research outputs for long-term discovery and reuse.
seeks_refutation: No — focuses on preservation and accessibility rather than validity assessment.
source_name: Wilkinson, M.D., et al. (2016). The FAIR Guiding Principles for scientific data management and stewardship. Scientific Data, 3, 160018.
